Q1: Honestly, I didn't have the most structured routine. I'd usually cram in study sessions when I had gaps between my extracurriculars and other commitments. Most of my studying happened in bursts—I'd find a few hours here and there, maybe late at night or on weekends. I'm pretty visual, so I'd often sketch out diagrams and mind maps rather than just reading through materials linearly. It wasn't consistent week to week, but I made it work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. Early in the semester I was pretty relaxed about it, but as deadlines approached I'd ramp up significantly. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, other weeks I'd put in serious hours. My attendance wasn't great because I found I could learn more efficiently on my own time than sitting through lectures where I'd just zone out.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing when something was due. I'd also log in if I needed to download specific materials or check my grades. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would prompt me to go in. It wasn't really a habit—more like responding to specific needs as they came up.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd use it when I had to, but it always felt more like a tool to access what I needed rather than a place to hang out or engage with. The closest it got to routine was checking assignment deadlines every few days, but even that felt more obligatory than natural.
Q5: The video content was decent when I actually watched it, especially if it had good visual explanations. I'm a visual learner so diagrams, charts, and demonstrations stuck with me way better than walls of text. The assignment submission portal was obviously essential. I also found the discussion posts occasionally useful when other students explained concepts in different ways than the instructor.
Q6: I mostly ignored the longer reading materials posted on the LMS. I'd skim them or find alternative explanations elsewhere that were more visual or concise. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant—like they were padding the course rather than adding real value. If something looked boring or text-heavy, I'd usually find a YouTube video covering the same topic instead.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content, practice problems with immediate application, and anything that felt efficient rather than busywork. I got bored easily with things that felt like they were just ticking boxes rather than actually teaching me something useful.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like suggestions I could take or leave. Required ones I'd do regardless of how I felt about them, but optional ones had to really sell themselves to me. If an optional activity looked interesting or seemed like it would genuinely help, I'd do it. But most of the time, optional meant 'skip unless you're desperate' in my mind.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked with them offline—PDFs, lecture slides, that kind of thing. I also used external resources constantly—YouTube tutorials, other textbooks, study apps. My actual learning happened more off the LMS than on it. I'd take handwritten notes and create my own visual summaries that I'd review repeatedly.
Q10: Most of my exam prep happened completely offline. I'd print out key materials or have them saved locally and just work through problems on paper. I'd also study with a couple of friends sometimes, going over concepts together, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere in the system. A lot of my best learning happened while I was drawing out concepts on whiteboards or paper.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs once and then never go back to the LMS for them. I preferred having everything locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the LMS interface. Once I had what I needed, the LMS became irrelevant for that material. I'd review those downloaded files multiple times over the semester.
Q12: Mid-semester I hit a wall where everything just felt tedious and boring. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and the course content wasn't grabbing me. My attendance dropped because I felt like I could learn the material faster on my own. The CBT sessions I did actually helped me push through that period and reframe how I was approaching things.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or tired from other commitments, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd prioritize assignments that were due immediately and let everything else slide. Sometimes I'd be too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully, so I'd just do the bare minimum to stay on track. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS only got attention when absolutely necessary.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just scroll through without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—thinking about other stuff I had to do or just feeling bored with the content. I'd technically be 'studying' but not really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine engagement.
Q15: I'd postpone discussion posts and optional readings constantly. They felt less urgent than assignments with clear deadlines and grade impacts. Even when I knew engaging more would probably help me learn better, I'd prioritize things that felt more immediately important. It's not that I didn't care—I just had limited time and energy to allocate.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd work backwards from due dates and plan my study sessions around them. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't engage with material at all. The closer a deadline got, the more intensely I'd use the LMS and related materials. It's not ideal, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to keep doing what was working. When I got high marks back, it validated my approach even if it wasn't conventional. Constructive criticism helped me adjust for future assignments. But honestly, I didn't check feedback as often as I probably should have—usually just looked at the grade and moved on unless I did poorly.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I needed but also maybe abused a bit. Having materials available online meant I could skip classes and still access everything. The assignment schedule was clear enough that I could plan around it. But the structure also made it easy to disengage from the live components since I knew I could catch up later.
Q19: Having visual materials and downloadable resources made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when content was text-heavy or when I had to be online at specific times. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—like engagement for engagement's sake rather than genuine learning. Clear assignment rubrics definitely helped me focus my efforts efficiently.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to figure out what's expected without actually doing the work themselves. Or they could be struggling and spending lots of time confused without making progress. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—you can spin your wheels a lot without getting anywhere meaningful.
Q21: That's basically me. I performed well because I studied efficiently outside the LMS, not because I was constantly logged in. I knew what I needed to learn, found the best ways to learn it—often external resources—and focused my energy there. The LMS was just a portal to get materials and submit work, not where my actual learning happened.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and how often, which gives a surface-level picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just clicking through. They definitely can't capture offline studying, external resources, or the mental effort you're putting in.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, real engagement happens when I'm creating visual summaries or explaining something to a friend—active processing rather than passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on external resources and offline studying. It wouldn't capture the visual learning strategies I used or the peer discussions outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling bored a lot of the time, I still pushed through and performed well because I'm resilient like that. The data tells a story, but it's not the whole story.
